title_tesim: Grocery store set for Charlie Murray and George Sidney film, circa 1930-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Charlie Murray. George Sidney.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Charlie Murray and George Sidney starred in films together in the 1920's and 1930's. They appeared in one full-length film produced by Columbia Pictures: Around the Corner (1930), as well as six comic shorts produced by Columbia Pictures and released in 1934.

The set consists of a counter with a scale on it, as well as boxes of Baby Ruth candy bars and Butterfingers. There are also shelves filled with goods (S and W grapefruit, salmon, corn, and asparagus, Willapoint oysters, Monarch prunes).

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Film still from the Columbia Pictures musical comedy All Ashore (1953). Featured at center are singer Dick Haymes (at left) and dancer/actor Ray McDonald. Both men are viewed holding sailor’s caps in their hands. They, along with the other performers, wear sailor uniforms. The setting replicates the sailors’ quarters of a Navy ship, complete with bulkhead light fixtures, and multiple bunk beds stacked three deep (hanging from linked chains).

title_tesim: Francis Ryan and Grady Sutton, actor, 1941
summary_tesim: Text from related ephemera: Frank Ryan + Grady Sutton. ["They ..." crossed out] She Knew All the Answers. [Image ark no. 21198/zz002jdgw9]
Text from negative enclosure: Frank Ryan. Grady Sutton.
Handwritten on negative: S-5.
subject_topic_tesim: She knew all the answers (Motion picture)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: According to the American Film Institute (AFI) catalog the filming of She Knew All the Answers took place from January 23, 1941 to March 3, 1941. The film was released on May 14, 1941.

On the negative enclosure and related ephemera, the man on the left is identified as Frank Ryan. The only person with a similar name related to She Knew All the Answers is Francis Ryan, who, according to the American Film Institute (AFI) catalog contributed dialogue to the film.

title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, caption on back of photo, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

Text on back of photo: [Typescript:] Jessie Ralph as a drunken sot and an excellent production it is too. Miss Ralph has appeared in one picture thus far but won immediate friends with audiences all over the country. The picture was “Child of Manhattan.” She will next be seen in “Biddy” with Richard Cromwell. [Stamped:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
ark:/21198/zz002jdkc0 (front of photo)
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

description_tesim: According to the American Film Institute (AFI) catalog, Sailor's Holiday was filmed between November and December of 1943, released February 1944.

Top row (l to r): John Moss (Errol Flynn), Gino Corrado (Leo Carrillo), Paul Bradley (Ronald Colman), Roy Thomas (Spencer Tracy), Rodney Bell (Charles Laughton). Center row (l to r): Linda Landi (Frances Langford), Pamela Drake (Veronica Lake), Mary Miner (Irene Dunne), Ezelle Poule (ZaSu Pitts), Margaret Bryson (Loretta Young), Elaine Waters (Mary Astor). Bottom row (l to r): Len T. Brixton (Edward G. Robinson), Robert Katcher (Peter Lorre), Sydney Chatton (Paul Muni), Rod Rogers (James Cagney).

title_tesim: Jessie Ralph, actress, circa 1933-1934
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Jessie Ralph. Prints.

On back of photo: [Press tag:] Jessie Ralph, Columbia character actress, in an excellent pose of a drunken sot made by the portrait artist, Carl De Voy. Miss Ralph is one of Broadway’s best character actresses and is now under Columbia contract. She will co-star with Richard Cromwell in “Biddy,” a sentimental tale of a mother and her son. [Photographer stamp:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark:/21198/zz002jdkq5 (back o f photo)
Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: It appears that “Biddy,” starring Jessie Ralph and Richard Cromwell, was not produced. The actors never appeared together on film.
Jessie Ralph began as a theater actor, and transitioned into film work in the 1930’s when she was in her 70’s. She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures spanning the years 1933-1934.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, collection of childhood photos, copy prints, [rephotographed] circa 1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Connie Towers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).
Eight copy print photographs of Constance Towers (b. 1933) and another young girl (likely Lucille Towers, listed with Constance in the 1940 U.S. census). Prints possibly rephotographed circa 1955.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Constance Towers, singer and actress, in childhood photos, copy prints, [rephotographed] circa 1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Connie Towers.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Two copy print photographs of Constance Towers (b. 1933) and another young girl, likely Lucille Towers (listed with Constance in the 1940 U.S. census). Prints possibly rephotographed circa 1955.

Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

Singer and actress Constance "Connie" Towers’ stage and screen career spans six-decades and includes radio work, nightclub performances (supporting and headlining), starring roles in Broadway musicals, guest appearances on episodic television and roles in several television soap operas. She appeared in two Columbia Pictures productions: Bring Your Smile Along (1955) and Fast Forward (1985).
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Elliott Clawson, screenwriter, circa 1930-1939
summary_tesim: On back of photo: [Press tag:] Elliott Clawson, who began writing for the screen in 1913, has to his credit such screen successes as “The Kaiser, the Beast of Berlin”, “The Phantom of the Opera”, “West of Zanzibar”, “The Road to Mandalay”, “The Leatherneck”, “Skyscraper”, “Sal of Singapore”, “Body and Soul”, “Earthbound”, “Those Who Dance” and “The Little Shepherd of Kingdom Come”. Ince, Goldwyn, Fox, M. G. M., Pathe, are a few of the studios that have been clamoring for his work in recent years. He has written five originals for Bill Boyd and three for Lon Chaney. In collaboration with Howard Higgin, noted writer and director, Clawson is now developing a sea mystery story that will give Jack Holt his greatest starring role. The story is entitled “The 13th Man”.
Text from negative enclosure: Elliott Clawson.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Elliott Clawson did not write any screenplays that were produced by Columbia Pictures, including "The 13th Man." The date of the image is based on the fact that the last of the films mentioned in the text was released in 1929.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: C. Bakaleinikoff, musical director, circa 1933
summary_tesim: On back of photo: [Press tag:] Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff. “Background music” for many of the most important motion pictures produced in Hollywood is composed or arranged by Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff, who once directed the Imperial Orchestra in Moscow. He is now attached to the Columbia studios, and scored the currently successful “Cocktail Hour,” as well as the unreleased picture, “Lady for a Day.” Recently he was guest conductor of the Philharmonic Orchestra of Los Angeles, by courtesy of Columbia. . [Stamp:] Please credit Carl De Voy, portrait artist, Columbia Studios.
Text from negative enclosure: Constantine [sic] Bakaleinikoff (music).

title_tesim: Lawrence Pohle (probably), writer, circa 1936-1937
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Lawrence Poble [sic, probably "Pohle"]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Lawrence Pohl was writer for movies and theater. He is credited as a contract writer for the movie Breakfast for Two (1937), a story writer for The Big Shot (1937) and Lets Make a Million (1936) (AFI catalog). He co-wrote three plays which were produced from 1934 to 1941: "Preface to Love" with Thomas Ahearn, "Alley Cat" with Alan Dinehart, and "Wise Girl" with Betty Laidlaw. He also co-wrote two screenplays with Thomas Ahearn that were not produced: "Let's be Candid," and "Take the Heir," the latter purchased by R.-K.-O.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam, author, circa 1932
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Nina Wilcox Putnam (writer).
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Nina Wilcox Putnam was an author who occasionally wrote for the movies. Though she did not write any Columbia Pictures films that were produced, according to the Los Angeles Times article, "Woes of Rich Will Be Told" [21 May 1932: A7], Ms. Putnam was hired by Columbia to write the screenplay for the adaptation of "No More Orchids."

title_tesim: Peggy Hamilton modeling a white ermine coat, 1930
summary_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: R 10
Text from negative enclosure: Mme. Matzenauer [mis-filed]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This another photograph of Peggy Hamilton in the same coat appears in Peggy Hamilton’s Los Angeles Times feature "Fashions," December 28, 1930, pg. H3.
Peggy Hamilton modeling a white Russian ermine coat from Colburn's Exclusive Fur Shop of South Flower Street in Los Angeles, over an evening gown.

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character of Tilly in the play "The Hoop of Gold," 1883
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The role of Tilly was Ms. Robson's theatrical debut. The show was performed at the Grand Opera House in Brooklyn, New York.
The text on the image is cut off, but based on the text from image ark no. 21198/zz002jbwkj it most likely reads, "'Tilly' in Hoop of Gold."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-23. Realisation. Bendrix (Van Johnson) and Sarah (Deborah Kerr) suddenly realise there is no turning back after their confession of love for each other, in this dramatic scene from David E. Rose’s powerful production of “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Johnson and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.

The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk. description_tesim: Portrait of S. K. Lauren.

title_tesim: Peter Cushing and Van Johnson in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Related press tag: Cor3-7 Tension. Henry Miles (Peter Cushing), waits tensely while Bendrix (Van Johnson), reads a letter from a private detective agency about his wife’s movements, in this scene from David E. Rose’s compelling drama “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills, with Peter Cushing heading the top featured cast. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis, and directed by Edward Dmytryk, with David E. Rose as Executive Producer. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful best-selling Graham Greene story.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr
Handwritten on photo: COR3-7A

title_tesim: Press tag describing a scene from The End of the Affair, circa 1955
summary_tesim: Press tag: Cor3-22. Confession. Both Sarah (Deborah Kerr) and Bendrix (Van Johnson) confess their love for each other, without knowing they will become entangled in a strange web of emotional disturbances, in this tender scene from David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson, and John Mills. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” which Columbia Pictures is to release world-wide, is based on the best-selling novel by Graham Greene. The screenplay was written by Lenore Coffee. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Van Johnson as Maurice Bendrix in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Handwritten on photo: COR3-P1A

Related press tag: Cor3-P1. Serious Role. Hollywood’s Van Johnson has one of the outstanding roles of his career as Bendrix, tormented, embittered writer who becomes involved in a passionate love affair with a married woman, in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Deborah Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new role, a complete departure from Van’s previous successes, marks the first time he has appeared before the cameras in the British studio. Produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk, “The End of the Affair,” is based on Graham Greene’s world-famous and best-selling novel of the same name and is to be distributed world-wide by Columbia Pictures. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: Deborah Kerr and Van Johnson in The End of the Affair, 1955
summary_tesim: Text from related press tag: Cor3-11 Passionate Moment. Sarah (Deborah Kerr) and Bendrix (Van Johnson) cling desperately to their few moments of happiness during their brief, but passionate, relationship in David E. Rose’s “The End of the Affair,” which co-stars Miss Kerr, Van Johnson and John Mills. The new Coronado Production, for world-wide release by Columbia Pictures, is produced by David Lewis and directed by Edward Dmytryk. Lenore Coffee wrote the screen adaptation of this unusual and powerful, best-selling, Graham Green story. Still by Lawrence [sic] Ridley.
Handwritten on photo: COR3-11A
Text from negative enclosure: Deborah Kerr

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, as the character Miss Ashford in the play "The Private Secretary," 1890
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The date has been determined based on articles in The New York Times, as well as Ms. Robson's entry in Who's Who in Music and Drama (1914).

May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The text on the image is cut off, but based on the text from image ark no. 21198/zz002jbwj1 it most likely reads, "Miss Ashford in Private Secretary."
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986

title_tesim: May Robson, actress, New York, circa 1883-1885
summary_tesim: Text from negative enclosure: May Robson. Nitrate.
Handwritten on negative: WL.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key books. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: May Robson began her career on the stage in 1883, working both on Broadway and with touring companies. In the 1920's she transitioned into films. She continued acting well into her 80's and was nominated for an Oscar for her work in Lady for a Day (1933). She appeared in four films produced by Columbia Pictures, spanning the years 1933-1937.
The printed signature at the bottom of the image reads, "Sarony" and next to that is the address, "37 Union Sqr N."

The date of the image has been determined by the fact that May Robson began her theater career in 1883 and Napoleon Sarony (photographer of the original image) moved his photography studio away from 37 Union Square in 1885.
member_of_collections_ssim: Columbia Pictures Stills and Key Books, 1947-1989, bulk 1950-1986
